title: Pregnancy scanning and flushing ewes
subtitle: Using scan results to manage feeding in late pregnancy and improving conception rates through flushing.
excerpt: Pregnancy scanning is one of the highest-return husbandry actions a small flock owner can take. Knowing which ewes carry singles, twins, or triplets before the last six weeks of pregnancy allows feed to be targeted where it is needed most. Flushing before tupping improves conception rates and twin percentages without significant cost.
sourceNotes: AHDB Beef and Lamb: scanning and managing pregnancy nutrition (2022), ahdb.org.uk. DEFRA Sheep Welfare Codes, gov.uk. SAC Consulting: flushing ewes for improved reproductive performance.

glossaryTerms.definition: Ultrasound examination of ewes 45 to 90 days after the ram was removed, to determine pregnancy status (in lamb or empty) and litter size (singles, twins, triplets). Results are used to group ewes by expected nutritional demand in late pregnancy.

The practice of increasing the plane of nutrition for ewes two to three weeks before tupping and continuing through the first three weeks of the breeding period. A rising body condition stimulates the release of more eggs at ovulation, increasing the proportion of twins in the lambing.

A numeric scale from 1 (emaciated) to 5 (obese) used to assess the fat cover of a sheep by palpating the spine and ribs. At tupping, ewes should be at BCS 3 to 3.5. At lambing, the aim is BCS 2.5 to 3 for single-bearing ewes, 2.5 for twin-bearing ewes.
A ewe confirmed by scanning to be not in lamb. Identified and managed separately from the pregnant group. Empty ewes may be sold or managed to reduce wintering cost depending on the cause of failure.

content.text: Two management tools, well-timed, do more for lambing outcomes on a small flock than any other intervention: flushing ewes before tupping to improve conception rates, and
at 45 to 90 days of gestation to split the flock for targeted feeding. Both require modest planning. Neither needs specialist equipment beyond a raddle on the tup and a scan booking with the vet.
of the flock 6 to 8 weeks before tupping. Ewes below BCS 2.5 need supplementary feeding to reach BCS 3 before the ram goes in.

is most effective on ewes that are moderately thin and improving, rather than on ewes already at peak condition. Moving ewes onto fresh pasture two to three weeks before tupping is the classic approach. If grass is limited, a small daily supplement of 200 g of rolled barley achieves the same rising-plane effect.

Note the date the ram was introduced and the date he was removed. Scanning is most accurate at 45 to 90 days of gestation. Book a scanning visit accordingly. Mobile pregnancy scanning services for small flocks are available in most UK rural areas; your vet can scan if no dedicated service is nearby. The cost is small relative to the feed savings from identifying litter size.
The scanner's assistant marks each ewe with a spray or paint stick: single, twins, triplets, or

. Separate the flock into at least two groups after scanning: singles (lower nutritional demand) and multiples (higher demand). If practical, triplet-bearing ewes form a third group as they need the highest supplementary feeding. Empty ewes are identified for management or sale.

The last 6 weeks of pregnancy (roughly days 90 to 147) are when 75% of lamb growth occurs. Ewes carrying multiples need supplementary feed during this window: twin-bearing ewes require 200 to 300 g of supplementary concentrate per day; triplet-bearing ewes 400 to 500 g. Single-bearing ewes at BCS 3 can manage on good quality hay alone unless the weather is severe.

The main benefit of scanning is knowing which ewes to feed more. Singles at good condition need no concentrate; ewes carrying multiples do. In a flock of 20, the difference between feeding all ewes the same and feeding based on scan results saves a significant quantity of concentrate over the last six weeks of pregnancy.
